title: Will Q2 earnings season be a sell-the-news catalyst for stocks broadly?

Happy Monday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Delta,
presenting our macro minute for Monday,
July 13th, 2026. As always, we'll start
with the executive summary from today's
lead-off morning note, so let's dive
right in. Today's key macro question is,
will Q2 earning season be a
sell-the-news catalyst for stocks
broadly?
The short answer is the risk of this
likely transitory bearish outcome is
much higher than consensus currently
expects because of the rising
probability of negative revisions to AI
CapEx projections occurring concurrently
with the Fed they may opt to play action
pass, i.e. tighten cyclically, to set up
the run, i.e. ease structurally. Refer
to the monetary policy section of our
July 2026 macro scouting report and our
July 11th Around the Horn webcast for
more details.
In terms of analog nuance, in Korea, SK
Hynix fell a record 15% and Samsung
Electronics shed almost 11% dragging the
KOSPI down 9% today. Bucking this trend
was Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing
Company whose shares closed 1% higher
today after the company posted inline
36% quarterly sales growth.
Five of the six biggest US banks report
Tuesday opening earning season. S&P 500
index profits likely climbed 24% through
June per Bloomberg Intelligence. That
result ranks near a record high as
excluding post-recession rebounds.
Indeed, consensus expectations for S&P
500 sales and earnings growth continue
to boom, especially when we compare
robust expectations for 2027 that are
expected to compound on top of historic
late-cycle growth rates in 2026.
The current expected three-year forward
CAGR of S&P 500 per share equals 15%.
This level makes absolutely no sense for
an economy that is not emerging from
recession. The trailing three-year
average growth rate of S&P 500 earnings
equals 14%, which compares to 3% in the
third quarter of 2022, or third quarter
of 2000, and two, my apologies, minus 2%
in 4Q09, and 6% 1Q2021.
What do those
those growth rates and and time have in
common that
that is not shared by the current
dynamic? Well, I'll tell you right now
if you guessed those were emerging from
recession and this is not, you've
guessed correctly. All told, Wall Street
is betting that AI is transformational
for corporate profitability on an
accelerated timeline that justifies the
concentration risk in investors'
portfolios.
Are we in a bubble?
Yes.
>> [laughter]
>> Yes, we're in an AI bubble according to
the historic allocation to stocks on the
US household sector balance sheet and
the historic concentration of related
sectors in the US and global stock
markets. Does being in an AI bubble mean
investors should immediately sell
stocks? No. The most optimal time to
sell is when KISS and Dr. Mo begin
selling.
Elsewhere, Fed chair Fed Chairman Kevin
Warsh testifies before Congress Tuesday
and Wednesday for the first time
as Fed chair. It coincides with
Tuesday's June CPI and Wednesday's June
PPI releases. Forecasters expect both to
show relief after recent price spikes.
Still, the two-year nominal Treasury
yield reached an early 2025 peak amid
rising energy prices stoking Fed rate
hike bets. The rate Fed rate hike bets
Fed funds futures are now pricing in a
hike on September 16th and another hike
on January 27th of next year. So, as
always, we'll wrap up with a question
from our community. This one's titled

not trying to get out over my skis. I
just want to grow in knowledge. So
excellent question, happy to help here.
So the first thing I'll say is there are
five different conditions. What Dr. Mo
is is is a risk management overlay for
investors who are not following the
standard KISS model portfolio. So
obviously all of our professional
investor clients we have you know hedge
funds, mutual funds, insurance funds,
pension funds, family offices, uh
uh investment advisors, you know, those
types of professional clients we serve.
And then obviously we serve the
thousands of retail investors around the
world as well with the same signals, the
same tools. Everyone has access to the
same information. They just go to the
buffet and pick out what they want and
put it on their tray. That's for their
for its own, you know, investment
preferences and and risk tolerance etc.
So what Dr. Mo does is it allows
investors to to customize outside of the
standard KISS which we know is, you
know, when it's fully invested 60%
stocks, 30% gold, 10% Bitcoin.
You know, obviously it's not fully
invested at the current juncture in
terms of the reduction in risk, uh the
the side stepping of risk that we've
effectively done in gold and and
Bitcoin.
And so investors are, you know, using
Dr. Mo which has features which has
proper trade signals across, you know,
70
plus I want to say it's about 80 maybe
it's even 80 but roughly about 80
different factors at this point across
US equity sectors, equity factors,
geographies, fixed income sectors and
macro exposures. And so you know,
there's five different conditions that
an asset can be in from the perspective
of Dr. Mo in terms of the the risk
management, in terms of the signal, the
proper trade signal that Dr. Mo
produces. So let's just look at our you
know, let's pick you know, friend
Bitcoin here as an example. And so what
Dr. Mo is designed to do is to tell you
exactly what you should be doing in your
portfolio as it relates to how much risk
you want to take at the position sizing
level. That's a choice that you need to
make. So you know, you Dr. Mo say hey,
it's a good time to be a long max
position in a particular asset. Dr. Mo
will also say it's it's good time to be
a long half position in a particular
asset. Long max position being the dark
green here. Long half position being the
light green there. Dr. Mo will tell you
it's a good time to be no position in
the asset. And Dr. Mo will also tell you
if it's a good time to be a half
position, half short position in an
asset or full short position in an
asset, which would be the red or the
orange bars in these in these charts.
And so, you know, how does Dr. Mo
determine those those those proper trade
signals? It's a combination of
incorporating our market regime now
casting process to determine the the
target the
target exposures and the volatility
targeting for each of these assets in
terms of orienting the delta of the
signal. And then the bottom-up
percentage of overlay into Dr. Mo and
Kiss is the volatility adjusted momentum
signal for the asset itself. So,
obviously if the asset's volatility
adjusted momentum signal is confirming
what the Dr. Mo regime orientation is
suggesting, then you're going to have a
max position either to the long side or
short side. And if the the signal the
Vime signal is neutral, then it's you're
going to have a half position either to
the long side or short side. And then
obviously when the signals are
incongruent, i.e., let's say you got a
bearish signal in Bitcoin and in a
bullish market regime as we currently
are in, then Dr. Mo will recommend no
position because again you're not
getting that confirmation from a
top-down and bottom-up percentage of
overlay perspective from a vol targeting
and dynamic position sizing uh
perspective. So, hopefully that's
helpful. And so obviously, you know,
long half position, you know, long short
half position, long max position, short
max position,
no position. You You still have to make
as an investor as a DIY investor, you
have to determine exactly what your max
position is for any given asset or asset
class before you can actually, you know,
effectively use this analysis. We can't
determine that for you. That's a That's
a personal
question and a personal choice. What
we're doing is the hard part of the
actual delta and the risk management of
the signal. And so you just have to
decide, you know, exactly what a what
